title: NPR: Federal Probe of Minneapolis Shootings Stalled; State Blocked from Evidence Including Agent's Training Records
excerpt: NPR reported that federal investigations into the three ICE/CBP shootings in Minneapolis remained opaque months after the incidents, with Minnesota officials saying the federal government had withheld evidence including victims' cell phones and cars, and had physically blocked state investigators from accessing shooting scenes. State officials specifically asked about ICE agent Jonathan Ross's training history and could not obtain it.
content:
On April 10, 2026, NPR reported that more than two months after the fatal shootings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti and the nonfatal shooting of Julio Sosa-Celis, the status of federal investigations into all three incidents remained unclear, and state authorities said they had been effectively shut out of accessing evidence.

  • Minnesota state officials told NPR that the federal government had given them 'little indication that the federal probes are progressing.' DHS's only response regarding Renee Good was that the matter 'remains under investigation.'
  • Evidence the state said it could not access included Alex Pretti's cell phone, Renee Good's car (which state officials said was 'shrink-wrapped in an FBI warehouse and has never been examined'), and personnel information about ICE agent Jonathan Ross, who shot Good.
  • State officials specifically asked: 'When was [Jonathan Ross] trained? What was he trained to do? What's in his personnel file?' — and received no response from federal authorities.
  • In late March, Minnesota and Hennepin County sued the Trump administration, accusing it of withholding evidence in all three shootings. State officials said federal agents had first agreed to work cooperatively, then took unilateral control of the evidence.
  • DHS admitted in its NPR statement that two officers involved in the Sosa-Celis shooting 'appeared to have made untruthful statements' and had been placed on administrative leave — the first public acknowledgment of misconduct in connection with the three Minneapolis shootings.
'For instance, Jonathan Ross, when was he trained? What was he trained to do? What's in his personnel file?' — question posed by Minnesota state officials to federal authorities, with no response received.
mediaPlaceholder: Timeline graphic showing the three Minneapolis shooting incidents alongside the status of federal and state investigations months later
